text:
Many scholars during the 20th century cautioned against the use of rigid, static, or ontological and metaphysical readings of Hellenistic philosophical categories in their attempt to describe the nature of God within a Christian framework. Given the new spiritual and philosophical climate of apatheism and anatheism, this paper challenges contemporary pastoral theology to re-examine and reconsider the place of classical theological categories. I call for a major paradigm shift away from “omni-categories” such as omnipotence and omniscience and towards a “pathos-categories” that take into account and attempt to make sense of the suffering, dignity, and justice inherent to the human condition. Theopaschitic readings of the nature of God allow for a reinterpretation of the “rigid God-image” that scholars have inherited from the Greco-Roman world. Within this context, this paper proposes the idea of a “sustainable God,” one in which God’s *ta splanchna* are seen not as static attributes, but as a continuum of an infinite number of divine interventions, characterized by “infiniscience,” and through a pneumatology of “being-with,” understood as an expansive and life-giving activity.

**Keywords:** Pastoral theology, theopaschitism, pathos-categories, suffering, divine compassion, anatheism.
